On the Fannie side, the latest Multifamily Guide communications show a DUS Gateway release for credit facility refinances and conversions effective April 3. That is not a glamorous headline, but it does reinforce an important point. The agencies are still actively tuning their execution machinery around real borrower use cases instead of standing still. Pair that with the 2026 caps of $88 billion each for Fannie and Freddie, and the agency lane still looks like the deepest dependable source of multifamily liquidity.

The production numbers reinforce that point, even though they are backward-looking. Fannie Mae said in February that it provided nearly $74 billion of multifamily financing in 2025. Freddie Mac said in January that its 2025 multifamily production volume reached $77.6 billion. Those are not today’s closings, but they do show the scale of the platforms that borrowers are still relying on in 2026 when bank credit stays selective and private-label volatility hangs around.

The negative side of the multifamily story is just as important. The same KBRA report that showed broader CMBS pressure also showed that multifamily distress worsened materially in March, driven by newer-vintage loans. That is a warning that the strongest multifamily assets still finance well, but weaker lease-up stories, overlevered bridge deals, and loans that depended on smoother refinance conditions are still getting exposed.

HUD and FHA continue to matter in that environment. HUD said last month that FHA will propose leveling all multifamily mortgage insurance premium categories to 25 basis points, effectively removing the old green-pricing distinction and making the economics more uniformly competitive. That keeps FHA relevant for borrowers who care about leverage, amortization, and durable fixed-rate execution. The tradeoff is still timing. FHA can be very competitive on economics, but it still is not the fastest answer when a sponsor needs certainty in a hurry.

So the multifamily message this morning is that liquidity is still real, but hierarchy matters. New construction and well-sponsored development can still pull private credit. Strong stabilized assets still have agency, life company, and selective bank options. FHA still works where process can be managed. But weaker recent-vintage loans are still feeding distress data, and that is keeping the market disciplined.

Before we close, here is the markets snapshot.

The latest official Treasury close from Tuesday, April 7 put the two-year at 3.81 percent, the five-year at 3.95 percent, the ten-year at 4.33 percent, and the thirty-year at 4.90 percent.

The latest New York Fed publication schedule still points to an orderly SOFR base, but one that remains elevated enough to keep floating-rate debt expensive on an all-in basis.

KBRA said first-quarter private-label CMBS issuance reached $32.9 billion, while CRE CLO issuance reached $14.5 billion.

KBRA also said the private-label CMBS delinquency rate rose to 7.7 percent in March, with the distress rate at 10.3 percent.

And one thing to watch next is Thursday morning’s April 9 data slate, especially personal income and outlays. If the next inflation read reinforces the message from the New York Fed survey and keeps the front end pinned, lenders have every reason to keep leverage conservative and spreads firm even if the Iran story stays temporarily contained.

The bottom line this morning is that the market is still open, but it is still open on selective terms. Nationally, the geopolitical clock has been extended, not resolved, and inflation pressure is still the macro problem that refuses to leave. In commercial real estate debt, the Treasury curve remains restrictive across the front, middle, and long end. CMBS and CRE CLO issuance are active, but spreads are responding to macro stress. Debt funds are still writing checks, especially in multifamily development, but only for stories they really believe in. Agency capital remains the strongest broad multifamily lane. FHA is still economically relevant. And the gap between financeable deals and difficult deals is still widening.

That is your Debt Desk for Wednesday, April 8, 2026.

I will see you tomorrow.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for April 7: A Tuesday Deadline for Iran, A Firmer Inflation Threat, and CRE Credit That Is Still Open but Selective
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk opens with the national setup around President Trump’s Tuesday deadline for Iran, the market’s uneasy reaction in oil and equities, and a new Fed warning that higher gasoline prices could keep rate pressure alive. Then it moves into the latest official Treasury curve and SOFR print, fresh debt-market execution signals, CMBS stress data, multifamily financing and agency activity, HUD and FHA positioning, a concise markets snapshot, and one thing to watch.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Tuesday, April 7, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s start with the national picture before we move into commercial real estate debt.

The biggest story this morning is that the market is heading into a hard geopolitical deadline, not a vague one. The Associated Press reported Monday that President Trump widened his threat against Iran to include power plants and bridges and said his deadline for a deal would run to Tuesday evening. Tehran, for its part, rejected the earlier 45-day ceasefire concept and pushed its own proposal through intermediaries. So by this morning the key fact is not just that tensions remain high. It is that the next move may come on a clock the market can actually see. That matters well beyond foreign policy because the Strait of Hormuz is still the pressure point connecting war headlines to freight, fuel, inflation, and risk appetite across U.S. markets.

The second national story is how markets handled that pressure on Monday. AP’s market wrap showed stocks grinding higher into the close, but the more important signal was the tone underneath the tape. Oil swung around all day as traders tried to decide whether the next chapter is escalation or de-escalation. The S and P 500 still finished higher, the Dow finished higher, and the Nasdaq finished higher, but that does not read like relief. It reads like a market marking time in front of an event risk it cannot price cleanly yet. For listeners in the debt world, that matters because it tells you volatility does not need to show up as an outright selloff to tighten financial conditions. It can show up as hesitation, wider risk premiums, and a lower tolerance for weak stories.

The third national story is that inflation risk is no longer just an abstract side effect of the Iran story. It is now being discussed explicitly by the Fed. AP reported Monday that Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack said she can see a scenario where the central bank could actually raise rates if inflation stays persistently above target. That is a notable shift in tone. The reason is straightforward: if higher gasoline prices feed into broader inflation psychology while labor conditions stay decent, the Fed has less room to talk about cuts and more reason to keep policy restrictive. For commercial real estate, that is a direct transmission channel. It keeps the front end high, keeps floating-rate borrowers under pressure, and makes every refinancing conversation more sensitive to carry and spread.

So the national setup this morning is tight and very clear. The Iran deadline is real. Oil and risk markets are trading like they know it. And the Fed is signaling that an energy-led inflation impulse could keep policy tighter for longer, or even reopen the rate-hike conversation if the data worsens.

Now let’s move into the Debt Desk section.

The first thing to know this morning is that the latest official Treasury curve still says the market is open, but it is not offering a gift. The U.S. Treasury’s official April 6 close put the two-year at 3.84 percent, the five-year at 3.98 percent, the ten-year at 4.34 percent, and the thirty-year at 4.89 percent. That is only a modest change from Friday, but the shape still matters. The two-year staying in the high threes tells you the front end remains restrictive enough to keep floating debt expensive. The five-year just under 4 percent keeps intermediate fixed-rate executions alive, but only for deals with disciplined leverage. The ten-year in the mid-4s keeps permanent debt workable without making it easy. And the thirty-year just under 4.9 percent is the reminder that duration still costs real money.

That full-curve view matters more right now than obsessing over the ten-year alone. A lot of refinance decisions are still being made in the belly of the curve because borrowers are trying to decide whether to buy a few years of certainty, bridge to stabilization, or pay up for longer-term fixed debt. When the two-year, five-year, ten-year, and thirty-year all remain elevated at the same time, there is no obvious cheap lane. There are only lanes that are less punitive than others depending on the business plan.

SOFR reinforces that point. As of run time, the latest official New York Fed SOFR print available is still 3.66 percent for April 2, with the April 3 publication skipped under the Fed’s holiday schedule. So the latest verified print is not flashing a funding accident, but it is also nowhere close to loose money. For floating-rate borrowers, a mid-3s base rate still becomes a very different conversation once lender spread, reserves, extension tests, and future cap costs get layered on top. That is why the headline for floating debt remains stable but expensive, not stressed but easy.

Execution tone across lender buckets is still selective in exactly the way borrowers have been feeling for weeks. Banks are lending where the relationship matters, sponsorship is credible, and repayment does not depend on hero assumptions. Life companies still look like the cleanest answer for low-leverage, high-quality permanent loans, especially when the borrower wants certainty and can live with lower proceeds. CMBS is open, but the market is still charging for structure and property-type risk. And debt funds remain essential wherever the story involves lease-up, recapitalization, rescue capital, or a bridge to agency or FHA, but that flexibility is still coming with a real coupon and tighter covenants.

The securitized market numbers back that up. CRE Finance Council’s March 31 update said six transactions totaling $4.9 billion priced the prior week. It also showed conduit AAA spreads around plus 68 basis points, BBB-minus around plus 435, SASB AAA in a roughly plus 100 to plus 165 range depending on asset type, and CRE CLO AAA around plus 135 to plus 140. Those are functioning levels. They are not loose levels. The message is that the market will execute, but it still wants compensation for complexity and headline risk.

The stress data is still moving the wrong way, which is why lender posture has not loosened. Trepp reported on March 31 that the overall CMBS delinquency rate rose 41 basis points in March to 7.55 percent. KBRA followed on April 1 saying its private-label CMBS delinquency rate rose to 7.7 percent while the distress rate held at 10.3 percent. The part multifamily borrowers should not miss is that KBRA said multifamily distress jumped 92 basis points as 18 loans became delinquent, with a heavy concentration in 2021 through 2025 vintages. That is the market continuing to discover which recent-era assumptions do not survive higher-for-longer rates, insurance inflation, and weaker refinance proceeds.

Even with that backdrop, deals are still getting done, and that is important this morning because it separates liquidity from leniency. On April 6, JLL said it arranged credit-tenant-lease financing for a 200,000-square-foot Massachusetts State Police crime lab and training facility through the private placement bond market. That is not a generic office refinance. It is a highly structured execution for a defined use case, and it shows institutional capital still wants durable cash flow and clarity. Also on April 6, JLL announced the sale of a fully leased shallow-bay industrial portfolio in New Jersey. That matters because it is one more reminder that high-conviction industrial product still clears when occupancy, location, and sponsor quality line up.

In other words, the market is still financing conviction. What it is not financing is ambiguity at yesterday’s pricing.

Now bring that into multifamily, because this remains the deepest lending lane in commercial real estate even as the credit splits get sharper.

The cleanest recent example is still The Opal in West Valley City, Utah. JLL announced on April 1 that it arranged construction takeout financing for the 262-unit Class A property on behalf of TPG Angelo Gordon and Timberlane Partners. It is not a same-day headline, but it is still highly relevant because the deal fits the exact multifamily profile that continues to work: recently delivered, institutionally backed, and financeable enough to move from construction risk into longer-duration debt. That is the continuity story from yesterday, and it still holds this morning.

Agency capital remains central to that lane. FHFA’s 2026 multifamily caps remain 88 billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, with at least half of production required to be mission-driven. Fannie Mae said in February that it provided about 74 billion dollars of multifamily financing in 2025, up 34 percent year over year. Freddie Mac said in January that its 2025 multifamily production volume reached 77.6 billion dollars, up 17 percent. Those are backward-looking numbers, but they still matter because they define the scale of the liquidity machine borrowers are dealing with in 2026.

And there are newer operational signals too. Freddie Mac said on April 1 that its long-term financing facility business closed seven new facilities in 2025 and ended the year with 15 actively managed facilities. That tells you sophisticated sponsors are still leaning toward flexible, portfolio-style structures where that optionality makes sense. On the Fannie side, the multifamily guide showed an April 3 gateway release for credit facility refinances and conversions. That is not a splashy headline, but it is a useful signal that the agencies are still adjusting execution tools around real borrower needs instead of sitting still.

Agency securitization also remains supportive. CRE Finance Council said year-to-date agency issuance through March 31 reached 31 billion dollars, up 39 percent from the comparable period last year. That is one of the strongest pieces of current evidence that the multifamily liquidity channel is still running even while private-label CMBS stress keeps rising.

But the bifurcation inside multifamily is real and it is getting harder to ignore. Clean, stabilized multifamily still attracts bank, life company, agency, and in some cases CMBS attention. Transitional multifamily still has capital available, especially from debt funds and bridge lenders, but the terms are tighter and the milestones matter more. Weak deals, especially deals underwritten on aggressive rent growth or easy refinancing assumptions from the 2021 to 2025 period, are still getting exposed. That is the thread connecting The Opal on the positive side and the March delinquency data on the negative side.

HUD and FHA still deserve a real place in the stack under those conditions. HUD said last month that it would level multifamily mortgage insurance premium categories to 25 basis points, effectively removing the old pricing edge tied to green treatment and making the platform more uniformly competitive. That keeps FHA in play for borrowers who value longer amortization, higher leverage, and durable fixed-rate execution. The tradeoff is unchanged: process and timing still matter. FHA can win the economics. It does not usually win the speed contest.

So the multifamily message this morning is not that everything is fine. It is that there is still a very real hierarchy of financeability. Agency-ready assets are still in the best position. Transitional assets can still get bridged, but at a price. And weaker recent-vintage loans are continuing to feed CMBS stress statistics.

Before we close, here is the markets snapshot.

The latest official Treasury close from April 6 put the two-year at 3.84 percent, the five-year at 3.98 percent, the ten-year at 4.34 percent, and the thirty-year at 4.89 percent.

The latest verified SOFR print available at run time is 3.66 percent for April 2.

Trepp’s March CMBS delinquency rate is 7.55 percent.

KBRA’s March private-label CMBS delinquency rate is 7.7 percent, with distress at 10.3 percent.

CREFC said year-to-date agency issuance through March 31 was 31 billion dollars, up 39 percent from the same point in 2025.

And one thing to watch today is whether the market treats tonight’s Iran deadline as a headline event or a genuine financing event. If crude moves sharply higher and the front end of the Treasury curve refuses to ease, lenders do not need a full market break to get more conservative. They can simply keep spreads firm, lower leverage, and demand more structure. In this market, that is enough to change who gets a deal done and who does not.

The bottom line this morning is that the market is still open, but it is still open on adult terms. Nationally, the geopolitical clock is now visible, the inflation risk from higher energy prices is more explicit, and the Fed is not ruling out a firmer response if price pressure sticks. In commercial real estate debt, the Treasury curve remains restrictive across the front, middle, and long end. SOFR is stable but still expensive. CMBS is functioning, but stress is still climbing. Debt funds are still necessary but not forgiving. Multifamily remains the strongest financing lane, especially where agency or FHA can solve for duration and structure. And the gap between financeable deals and difficult deals is still widening, not narrowing.

That is your Debt Desk for Tuesday, April 7, 2026.

I will see you tomorrow.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for April 6: A Hotter Oil Tape, A Tight Funding Window, and Multifamily Finance That Still Has Lanes
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk opens with fresh national headlines on the rescue inside Iran, the new ceasefire push and oil shock, the White House budget fight, and the pharmaceutical tariff order. Then it moves into the latest available Treasury curve, the SOFR publication gap and pending Monday print, CMBS delinquencies, lender spreads and execution tone, multifamily refinancing and agency activity, plus HUD and FHA positioning.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Monday, April 6th, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s start with the national picture before we move into the desk.

The first story this morning is the one markets could not ignore overnight, and that is the combination of a dramatic U.S. rescue inside Iran and a fresh threat to the Strait of Hormuz. The Associated Press reported Sunday that U.S. forces completed a high-risk mission to recover the second crew member from the F-15E that was shot down last week, while President Trump was simultaneously escalating pressure on Tehran to reopen Hormuz. That matters because the rescue closes one immediate military crisis, but it does not close the larger risk trade. If anything, it reinforces that this conflict still has room to widen, and it comes at exactly the moment energy, shipping, and inflation expectations were already on edge.

The second national story is that diplomacy is not dead, but it is not in control yet either. AP also reported that mediators from Egypt, Pakistan, and Turkey delivered a 45-day ceasefire proposal that would pair a truce with the reopening of the Strait. As of early Monday morning, there was no clear acceptance from Washington or Tehran. So the real takeaway is not that peace is at hand. The takeaway is that the market now has to price two things at once: a real diplomatic off-ramp and a still-live chance of another escalation if that off-ramp fails.

Third, the domestic economic consequence is showing up first in energy. Overnight reporting from the Wall Street Journal and AP showed oil pushing higher again as traders reacted to the latest U.S. threats and the still-disrupted shipping route. For the broader economy, this is where the foreign policy story becomes a kitchen-table story. Higher oil means higher gasoline, more pressure on transportation-heavy sectors, and one more reason for rates markets to stay cautious even if growth data cools later this month.

The fourth story is Washington’s fiscal backdrop. The White House budget request released Friday is still the opening bid for the week ahead. Reuters and the Associated Press reported that the administration proposed a 10 percent cut in non-defense discretionary spending while asking for a major increase in defense spending for fiscal 2027. That is not law, and Congress will write its own bills, but it does frame the week. The debt-market implication is straightforward: more fiscal conflict, more attention on supply and deficits, and less room for anyone to assume rates volatility just fades away on its own.

And the fifth story to keep in view is trade policy, specifically the April 2 White House move on patented pharmaceuticals. The administration’s fact sheet and Reuters’ reporting make clear that branded pharmaceutical imports can face tariffs as high as 100 percent if companies do not strike pricing deals or onshore production. This is still early in implementation, but it is material because it adds another inflation-sensitive policy lever at the same time energy costs are already moving the wrong way.

So that is the national setup this morning. The rescue mission was a tactical success. The ceasefire proposal gives the market a possible off-ramp but not a resolution. Oil is still climbing. Washington opened a new budget fight. And tariff policy is back on the board in a way that could matter for costs and supply chains.

Now let’s move into the Debt Desk section.

The first thing to know this morning is that the latest official Treasury curve, from the Friday, April 3 close, still says commercial real estate borrowers have liquidity, but not relief. The two-year Treasury closed at 3.84 percent, the five-year at 3.99 percent, the ten-year at 4.35 percent, and the thirty-year at 4.91 percent. That curve matters because it tells you the pain is not isolated to one benchmark. The front end is still high enough to keep floating-rate debt from feeling easy, the belly of the curve is still high enough to challenge intermediate fixed-rate executions, and the long end is still expensive enough that duration is available only if the borrower can actually pay for certainty.

And on a Monday morning like this one, the shape matters as much as the level. The two-year under 4 percent says the market is not fully repricing into panic yet. The five-year just under 4 percent matters for a large share of real-world refinancing decisions, because that is still where a lot of borrowers are trying to land if they cannot make ten-year economics work. And the thirty-year brushing 4.9 percent is the reminder that permanent capital is there, but it is not generous.

SOFR is the next key point, and this morning it comes with a calendar wrinkle that borrowers need to understand. The New York Fed said in advance there would be no SOFR publication on Friday, April 3, because repo market participants were broadly expected to treat the day as a holiday. The Fed also said the next SOFR publication would come on Monday, April 6, reflecting Thursday, April 2 transactions. At the time this script is being written, that Monday morning print is not yet out. So as of run time, the latest official SOFR print remains 3.66 percent for Thursday, April 2.

That tells you something useful. Even with geopolitical stress and a volatile oil tape, the latest official SOFR print still describes a funding environment in the mid-3s rather than a fresh spike back into 4-handle panic. For floating-rate borrowers, that does not mean cheap money. It means the carry backdrop is manageable only if the rest of the capital stack is disciplined.

That is why execution tone matters more than broad statements about liquidity.

The market is still lending. JLL said last week that Mortgage Bankers Association league tables showed it arranged 98 billion dollars of debt originations in 2025, up 32 percent from 2024, and led origination for insurance companies, investor-driven lenders, and multifamily. That is a useful reminder that capital formation did come back once rates stabilized enough for sponsors and lenders to meet in the middle.

But the middle is still narrow. Banks remain active when the relationship matters, the sponsor is credible, and cash flow can withstand some wobble in lease-up or exit timing. Life companies are still the best answer for lower-leverage, cleaner, more permanent executions, and they remain competitive when the borrower is bringing durable income and realistic leverage. What they are not doing is stretching for volume.

CMBS is open too, but the spread picture still argues for discipline instead of victory laps. CRE Finance Council’s March 31 update showed conduit AAA spreads around plus 68 basis points, BBB-minus around plus 435, SASB AAA in roughly a plus 100 to plus 165 range depending on collateral, and CRE CLO AAA around plus 135 to plus 140. Those are workable levels. They are not loose levels. In other words, the securitized market is available for the right story, but it is still charging borrowers for credit risk, structure risk, and property-type uncertainty.

And the stress data continues to justify that posture. Trepp reported on March 31 that the overall CMBS delinquency rate rose 41 basis points in March to 7.55 percent. KBRA reported on April 1 that the private-label CMBS delinquency rate increased to 7.7 percent, while distress held at 10.3 percent. The most important point for this audience is that KBRA flagged a 92-basis-point increase in multifamily distress, with newly delinquent loans concentrated in 2021 through 2025 vintages. That is a big tell. The market is still finding new cracks in loans that were originated under a much friendlier assumption set.

Debt funds remain the group most willing to bridge that gap, and that is still true this morning. If the deal needs speed, flexibility, rescue capital, lease-up runway, or a path into agency or FHA later, debt funds are still essential. But the trade has not changed. The flexibility comes with higher coupons, tighter milestones, and far less tolerance for a fuzzy business plan. A debt fund can buy time. It usually will not buy forgiveness.

So the broad CRE debt takeaway this morning is simple: deals are getting done, but lenders are still sorting hard between assets that merely need capital and assets that need a new capital story.

Now bring that down into multifamily, because this is still the deepest financing lane in the market, even with new stress showing up in securitized credit.

The positive case is easy to see. Stabilized multifamily, newer product, strong sponsorship, and properties with honest operating assumptions still have multiple execution lanes. JLL’s April 1 announcement on The Opal in West Valley City is a clean example. Construction takeout financing got done on a 262-unit Class A community for TPG Angelo Gordon and Timberlane Partners. That is the kind of deal the market still likes: recently delivered, institutionally sponsored, and clear enough on the handoff from development risk to permanent or near-permanent debt.

Agency capital remains central to that lane. FHFA’s 2026 multifamily caps are 88 billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, with at least half of business still required to be mission-driven. Fannie Mae said in February that it provided approximately 74 billion dollars of multifamily financing in 2025, up 34 percent from 2024. Freddie Mac said its 2025 multifamily production volume totaled 77.6 billion dollars, up 17 percent year over year. Those are not just backward-looking statistics. They are the clearest reminder that the agencies still represent the most dependable liquidity stack in multifamily.

Freddie also gave the market a useful current signal on April 1. Its update on long-term financing facilities said it closed seven new facilities in 2025 and ended the year with 15 actively managed facilities. That matters because it says sophisticated borrowers are still looking for optionality, collateral flexibility, and portfolio management tools, not just plain-vanilla one-off quotes. In a choppier market, even strong borrowers want structure that can absorb change.

Agency securitization still supports that picture. CREFC said year-to-date 2026 agency issuance had reached 31 billion dollars through March 31, up 39 percent from the comparable 2025 period. That does not mean every multifamily borrower gets heroic proceeds. It does mean the machine behind agency lending is still very much running, and that continues to separate stabilized multifamily from weaker property types and weaker stories.

At the same time, the trouble spots are real. KBRA’s March report is a reminder that some newer-vintage multifamily loans are already running into trouble as tax assumptions, insurance costs, rent growth expectations, and refinance math collide with actual lender terms. That is why the market still feels bifurcated. Clean multifamily deals have options. Transitional deals have capital, but it is more expensive and more conditional. Weak deals are still discovering that extension risk is not the same thing as refinanceability.

HUD and FHA still deserve a real seat at the table in that environment. HUD announced its move to level multifamily mortgage insurance premium categories to 25 basis points, effectively eliminating the old green-price advantage and making the platform more uniformly cost-competitive. That keeps FHA relevant for borrowers who want longer amortization, durable fixed-rate proceeds, and stronger leverage than many private lenders are willing to offer. The tradeoff is still process and speed. FHA can be the right answer when economics and duration matter more than a fast closing clock.

Before we close, here is the markets snapshot.

As of the latest official Treasury close on Friday, April 3, the two-year was 3.84 percent, the five-year was 3.99 percent, the ten-year was 4.35 percent, and the thirty-year was 4.91 percent.

As of this recording, the latest official SOFR print available from the New York Fed is 3.66 percent for April 2. The next SOFR publication is due later Monday morning.

Trepp’s March CMBS delinquency rate is 7.55 percent.

KBRA’s March private-label CMBS delinquency rate is 7.7 percent, with distress at 10.3 percent.

Agency issuance year to date through March 31 stood at 31 billion dollars, up 39 percent from the same point last year, according to CREFC.

And one thing to watch today is whether the front end of the Treasury curve and the next SOFR print confirm that funding markets are staying orderly even while oil and geopolitical risk heat up again. If repo funding stays calm but crude keeps rising, lenders do not need a market freeze to get more conservative. They can simply keep spreads firm, trim proceeds, and demand cleaner business plans. For borrowers, that still feels restrictive.

The bottom line this morning is that the market remains open, but it is open on adult terms. Nationally, the rescue in Iran reduced one immediate uncertainty without removing the larger geopolitical risk, and energy is still transmitting that stress into the domestic economy. In debt markets, the whole Treasury curve matters, not just the ten-year. SOFR has not broken higher on the latest official averages, but floating-rate money is still expensive enough to punish weak structure. CMBS is functioning, but it is still priced for caution. Debt funds remain critical, but not cheap. Agency multifamily lending is still the cleanest lane for high-quality deals. And HUD and FHA remain competitive where borrowers can trade speed for structure.

That is your Debt Desk for Monday, April 6th, 2026.

I will see you tomorrow.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for April 5: Iran Risk, a Stronger Jobs Print, and CRE Credit That Still Demands Discipline
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk starts with fresh national headlines on the U.S. rescue mission in Iran, the March jobs report, the White House budget push, and the new pharmaceutical tariff order. Then it moves into the April 3 Treasury curve, the latest SOFR publication calendar, CMBS stress, lender execution across banks, life companies, CMBS and debt funds, plus multifamily activity across agencies and HUD/FHA.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Sunday, April 5th, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s start with the national picture before we move into the desk.

The biggest story this morning is still Iran, but the development is different from where we left it yesterday. Overnight into Sunday, the U.S. completed the rescue of the service member who had been missing after Iran shot down an American F-15E on Friday. That closes one immediate crisis point, but it does not lower the broader temperature. If anything, it underlines how dangerous this conflict has become. You now have a live reminder that this is not just a distant geopolitical headline. It is directly affecting U.S. aircraft, regional energy infrastructure, shipping routes, and the way markets have to think about risk going into the new week.

That matters because the macro setup underneath it is not soft enough to give investors an easy answer. Friday’s March jobs report from the Labor Department showed payrolls rising by 178,000, with unemployment at 4.3 percent. In other words, the labor market came into this latest round of geopolitical stress in better shape than expected. Health care, construction, and transportation all added jobs, while federal employment continued to decline. The simple version is that the economy did not arrive at this moment already rolling over. The harder version is that a stronger labor print and a hotter oil backdrop can pull rates expectations in opposite directions at the same time.

Washington added another layer on Friday with the White House budget request for 2027. The administration is asking Congress for roughly 1.5 trillion dollars in defense spending while cutting domestic programs by about 10 percent. Congress will ultimately write the bills, so this is an opening bid, not enacted policy. But it still matters because it frames the next fiscal fight around defense, immigration enforcement, and cuts to housing and other domestic accounts. In debt markets, the point is less about whether this exact budget passes and more about what it signals: bigger fiscal arguments, bigger supply questions, and more pressure on anyone hoping rates volatility will just quietly disappear.

The fourth story to keep on the board is the administration’s new pharmaceutical tariff order. The White House announced tariffs of up to 100 percent on some patented drug imports for companies that do not strike pricing deals or build in the United States. The implementation window stretches out from here, so this is not an immediate market shock in the same way the Iran story is. But it is a reminder that trade policy is still very much a moving part, and that corporate planning, health care costs, and supply-chain assumptions can all change quickly from here.

So that is the national setup this morning. The rescue in Iran solved one urgent problem without resolving the larger war risk. The jobs report showed resilience. The White House opened a new spending fight. And tariff policy is back in motion in a very visible way.

Now let’s move into the Debt Desk section.

The first thing to know is that the Treasury curve still is not giving commercial real estate borrowers a free pass. According to the U.S. Treasury’s April 3 close, the two-year ended at 3.84 percent, the five-year at 3.99 percent, the ten-year at 4.17 percent, and the thirty-year at 4.91 percent. That is a very usable curve if you are underwriting a high-quality asset with reasonable leverage and real cash flow durability. It is still a punishing curve if you need heroic refinance assumptions or if your business plan depends on cheap long-duration money showing up at exactly the right time.

And the shape of the curve matters as much as the headline ten-year. The two-year at 3.84 tells you the front end is still high enough to keep floating-rate debt from feeling painless. The five-year right at 4 reminds you where a lot of intermediate fixed-rate executions still have to clear. The thirty-year near 4.9 says duration is available, but it is not being handed out cheaply. If you want certainty, you still have to pay for it.

SOFR adds an important wrinkle this weekend. The New York Fed said in advance that there would be no SOFR publication on Friday, April 3, because repo market participants were broadly expected to treat the day as a holiday around the jobs report and Good Friday calendar. The next SOFR publication comes Monday and will reflect Thursday, April 2 transactions. So the practical takeaway this morning is that there is no fresh Friday print to trade off of. The latest official compounded readings still sit in the high-3.6 percent area, which means carry has become more manageable than it was at the tightest point of the cycle, but it is still expensive enough to force discipline on every floating-rate capital stack.

That is why execution tone matters more than broad statements about liquidity.

At the top level, the market is lending. The Mortgage Bankers Association said in February that commercial and multifamily borrowing in the fourth quarter of 2025 was up 30 percent from a year earlier, with depositories up 74 percent, investor-driven lenders up 46 percent, CMBS up 5 percent, GSE volume up 4 percent, and life companies up 1 percent. That is not a frozen market. It is a market that came back once rates stabilized enough for people to transact.

But the current market is also very clearly sorting winners from borrowers who still want last cycle leverage on this cycle’s pricing. Banks are lending when relationships matter, sponsorship is strong, and the debt service story is believable even if lease-up or exit timing drifts. Life companies are still very competitive on lower-leverage, cleaner permanent deals, especially when they can match long duration to stable cash flow. What they are not doing is stretching to win volume. They are winning by staying inside the box.

CMBS is open too, but the tone is still cautious rather than generous. CRE Finance Council’s March 31 update showed conduit AAA spreads around plus 68 basis points and BBB-minus around plus 435, while SASB AAA ranged roughly plus 100 to plus 165 depending on property type and CRE CLO AAA sat around plus 135 to plus 140. That is workable execution, not easy execution. The market is open for better collateral and still demanding real spread compensation further down the stack.

And the credit data supports that caution. Trepp said March CMBS delinquency rose to 7.55 percent. KBRA said private-label CMBS delinquency increased to 7.7 percent in March, with distress steady at 10.3 percent. The more important detail for this audience is that KBRA flagged a 92-basis-point increase in multifamily distress, with several newly delinquent loans coming from 2021 through 2025 vintages. That tells you the pain is not confined to old office stories. Some newer multifamily paper is also running into the wall where aggressive assumptions meet real taxes, real expenses, and real takeout markets.

Debt funds remain the group that can still bridge that gap, but they are doing it on terms that reflect the environment. If the deal is transitional, needs rescue capital, needs a lease-up runway, or needs a bridge into agency execution later, debt funds are still essential. What has not changed is the tradeoff. Flexibility is there, speed is there, and custom structure is there, but pricing is higher and milestones are tighter. A debt fund can solve a timing problem. It usually will not solve a credibility problem.

The bottom line on general CRE debt this morning is that deals are getting done, but only when the borrower accepts the market that exists instead of the one they wish still existed.

Now narrow that down to multifamily, because that is still the deepest financing lane in the market even with fresh stress showing up in securitized credit.

The healthiest part of the multifamily market still looks familiar. Stabilized deals, newer assets with clean operations, and properties that can stand up to honest expense assumptions still have multiple execution paths. A good recent example is JLL’s April 1 refinancing of The Opal, a 262-unit newly constructed Class A community in Utah, where construction takeout financing got done for TPG Angelo Gordon and Timberlane Partners. That is exactly the kind of transaction that tells you capital is available for good sponsorship and a clean handoff from construction risk into stabilized debt.

Agency capital remains central to that story. FHFA’s 2026 caps are still 88 billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, so the agencies have room to keep showing up in size. Fannie Mae said in February that it produced nearly 74 billion dollars of multifamily financing in 2025, up 34 percent from 2024. Freddie Mac said its 2025 production volume totaled 77.6 billion dollars, up 17 percent year over year. Those are not just historical brag lines. They are a reminder that the two biggest liquidity engines in multifamily are still fully in the game.

Freddie also gave the market a timely structural signal on April 1. In its customer update on long-term financing facilities, it said it closed seven new facilities in 2025 and ended the year with 15 actively managed facilities. That matters because sophisticated borrowers are still asking for flexibility, optional collateral management, and structures that can survive a choppy market instead of just a simple single-shot loan quote. In other words, even strong borrowers want optionality now.

Agency CMBS also keeps telling a constructive story. CREFC’s late-March data showed year-to-date 2026 agency issuance running 39 percent ahead of the comparable 2025 pace. That does not mean every multifamily deal clears at heroic proceeds. It does mean the market still has a large, active securitization machine behind agency lending, which is one reason stabilized multifamily keeps separating itself from shakier property types.

The other side of that story is that bridge and debt-fund capital still matter a lot in multifamily because not every asset is ready for agency execution on day one. Lease-up deals, moderate rehab stories, and assets still digesting elevated taxes, insurance, or concessions need more runway. That is where debt funds remain important, but also where discipline matters most. If the bridge loan does not have a credible path into agency, HUD, or a strong bank or life company refinance, then the borrower is just renting time.

HUD and FHA still deserve a seat at the table as well. HUD’s recent move to level multifamily mortgage insurance premium categories to 25 basis points made the channel more cost-competitive and removed some of the distortion around the old green premium structure. That keeps FHA relevant for borrowers who want long amortization, strong leverage, and a durable fixed-rate answer. The catch is the same as always: it is often a better answer on economics than on speed. So FHA remains a very good lane when the borrower values certainty of structure and duration more than a fast close.

Put that all together and the multifamily market still feels bifurcated in a very recognizable way. Clean deals have options. Transitional deals have capital, but it is expensive and conditional. Weak deals are learning that a refinancing plan is not the same thing as refinanceable collateral.

Before we close, here is the markets snapshot.

As of the April 3 Treasury close, the two-year was 3.84 percent, the five-year was 3.99 percent, the ten-year was 4.17 percent, and the thirty-year was 4.91 percent.

There was no SOFR publication on Friday, April 3, under the New York Fed’s reference-rate schedule, so the next update comes Monday and reflects Thursday repo activity.

Trepp’s March CMBS delinquency rate stands at 7.55 percent.

KBRA’s March private-label CMBS delinquency rate stands at 7.7 percent, with distress at 10.3 percent.

Agency support remains large, with 2026 multifamily caps set at 88 billion dollars for both Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

And one thing to watch next is simple: watch whether the market treats the stronger jobs report as the bigger story on Monday morning, or whether geopolitical risk and energy move back to the top of the board. If the labor market keeps looking resilient while oil and war risk stay elevated, lenders may not need dramatically wider spreads to get more conservative. They can just hold the line on structure and trim proceeds. For borrowers, that can feel just as restrictive.

The bottom line this morning is that capital is there, but it is still being offered on disciplined terms. Nationally, the risk backdrop is more volatile than it was a few days ago. In debt markets, the Treasury curve is still high enough to matter across the entire stack, not just at the ten-year. CMBS is functioning, but it remembers the pain in the system. Debt funds are active, but expensive. The agencies remain the cleanest answer for a lot of multifamily. And HUD is still credible when duration and structure matter more than speed.

That is your Debt Desk for Sunday, April 5th, 2026.

I will see you tomorrow.

item.title: Debt Desk — Debt Desk for April 4: Jobs Rebound, Iran Escalates, and CRE Debt Stays Selective
item.description: Today’s Debt Desk opens with fresh national headlines on the March jobs report, the escalating Iran conflict, the White House budget request, and DHS funding. Then it moves into the April 3 Treasury curve, latest SOFR context, CMBS stress data, lender execution across banks, life companies, CMBS and debt funds, plus multifamily agency and HUD/FHA signals.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Saturday, April 4th, 2026, and this is Debt Desk.

Let’s start with the national picture before we move into the desk.

The biggest new headline this morning is that the Iran story has become more dangerous, not less. Overnight into Saturday, the U.S. military kept searching for a missing service member after Iran brought down an American warplane on Friday. One crew member was rescued, but at least one remained missing as of Saturday morning, and that matters well beyond the military angle. This is the first confirmed loss of U.S. aircraft in Iranian territory during this war, and it raises the risk that markets now have to price a longer and more volatile conflict instead of a short campaign with a quick off-ramp. Oil, shipping, and broader risk sentiment are going to trade that possibility.

That backdrop made Friday’s jobs report more complicated than a simple good-news headline. The Labor Department said nonfarm payrolls increased by 178,000 in March and the unemployment rate held near 4.3 percent. That is a real rebound from February’s weak reading, and on the surface it says the labor market still has some resilience. But the report also came with softer labor-force participation and with the larger macro question still unresolved: how much damage do higher energy prices and war uncertainty do to hiring in April and May if this conflict drags on. So the takeaway is not that the economy is suddenly clear. The takeaway is that the labor market entered this latest geopolitical shock in better shape than feared.

Washington also added a new fiscal and political headline on Friday. The White House released a 2027 budget proposal that would push defense spending to roughly one point five trillion dollars while cutting domestic programs. Congress will write the actual spending bills, so this is not law. But it is still important because it shows how the administration wants to frame the next fiscal fight: more military spending, continued immigration enforcement spending, and sharper pressure on housing, health, and other domestic accounts. In a market already watching war costs, deficits, and Treasury supply, that budget message is not a side story.

And the Homeland Security funding story is still not fully put away. Republican leaders announced a path earlier this week to move past their internal split and fund most of the department first, with ICE and Border Patrol to be handled separately later. Some backpay has started to flow to affected workers, which has helped ease the airport-chaos angle. But the broader point remains the same. The operational problem is not completely solved, and the politics around DHS funding are still live heading into next week.

So that is the national setup this morning. The labor market came in stronger than expected. The war story got worse. The budget fight is getting bigger. And Congress still has unfinished business on funding.

Now let’s move into the Debt Desk section.

The first thing to know is that Friday’s Treasury curve did not give borrowers much relief. According to the U.S. Treasury’s April 3 close, the two-year was about 3.84, the five-year about 3.99, the ten-year about 4.17, and the thirty-year about 4.91. That is the print that matters going into Monday because it tells you the market is still demanding real term premium even after the jobs data and even with geopolitical stress running hot.

The shape matters as much as the level. The two-year staying in the high threes means floating-rate borrowers are still dealing with expensive carry even after the Fed’s easing cycle. The five-year just under four and the ten-year above four still make fixed-rate permanent debt workable only if leverage is reasonable and the asset story is clean. And the thirty-year near 4.9 tells you long-duration capital is not cheap. If a borrower wants to lock long today, they have to really want the certainty.

SOFR, meanwhile, is no longer the crisis variable it was during the peak tightening period, but it is still very much a carry variable. The New York Fed continues to publish SOFR one business day in arrears, and the latest available reading remains in the mid-3.6 percent range. The direction there is basically sideways to slightly easier from late 2025, but not easy in the way borrowers mean when they say easy. For bridge loans, construction debt, and any business plan that still leans heavily on floating-rate exposure, that means the benchmark is no longer the whole problem, yet it is still expensive enough that spread, reserves, and extension mechanics can decide whether a deal pencils.

That is why execution tone matters more than simple headline volume.

At a high level, there is capital in the market. The Mortgage Bankers Association said commercial and multifamily originations in the fourth quarter of 2025 were up 30 percent from a year earlier, with depositories, investor-driven lenders, CMBS shops, life companies, and the agencies all participating. So this is not a frozen market. But it is a market that is allocating capital selectively and asking harder questions before it commits.

Banks are lending where sponsorship is strong, deposits or relationships matter, and proceeds can be sized to realistic debt-service coverage. What they still do not want is loose leverage on assets with a thin operating story. Life companies remain a very serious option for top-tier permanent debt, especially on multifamily and industrial-style credits with lower leverage and durable cash flow. But life company money is still winning by saying no to deals that do not fit its box. That means low drama, lower leverage, and clean sponsorship are still the price of admission.

CMBS is open, but the data says nobody should confuse open with forgiving. Trepp reported on March 31 that the overall U.S. CMBS delinquency rate jumped to 7.55 percent in March. KBRA then said on April 1 that the delinquency rate in its private-label CMBS universe increased to 7.7 percent, with the overall distress rate steady at 10.3 percent. That combination tells you the securitized market is still functioning, but it is functioning with an active memory of what has gone wrong.

And the distress is rotating in ways borrowers need to respect. KBRA said multifamily distress increased by 92 basis points in March as 18 loans became delinquent, including a number of 2021 through 2025 vintages. That is important because it confirms the problem is not only old-cycle office baggage. Multifamily deals that depended on perfect tax treatment, aggressive leverage, or a too-optimistic takeout plan are now showing stress too.

The latest available spread snapshot points the same way. CREFC’s recent securitized debt update showed conduit AAA spreads around plus 68 basis points, BBB-minus around plus 435, and SASB AAA execution ranging roughly plus 100 to plus 165 depending on property type, while CRE CLO AAA was around plus 135 to plus 140. In plain English, the market is open, but lower in the stack there is no charity. Credit quality still shows up in price.

That brings us to debt funds, and debt funds remain essential. In this curve, debt funds are still the lane for transitional multifamily, lease-up, rescue capital, recapitalizations, and bridge-to-agency stories that traditional lenders either cannot or will not carry. But the borrower tradeoff is still the same as it has been for a while now. Flexibility is available, speed is available, and structure is available, but the coupon is higher and the milestones are tighter. The debt fund market is not there to solve an incoherent business plan. It is there to finance a believable one that needs flexibility.

The desk takeaway on commercial real estate more broadly is straightforward. Deals are getting done when the borrower is realistic about proceeds, timing, and refinance optionality. The market will finance durability. It will not finance denial.

Now narrow that down to multifamily, because multifamily remains the deepest and most important lane for a lot of listeners.

The multifamily story is still split between the haves and the have-nots. Stabilized assets with strong collections, believable expense control, and clear exit options are financeable. Transitional assets, new deliveries still proving rents, and tax- or capex-sensitive business plans can still get financed, but they usually need either bridge money, more equity, or a very explicit path into agency debt.

The agencies are still central to that path. FHFA set the 2026 multifamily caps at 88 billion dollars each for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, which leaves plenty of room for both enterprises to keep providing liquidity this year. Fannie Mae said in February that it provided approximately 74 billion dollars of multifamily financing in 2025, up 34 percent from 2024. Freddie Mac said its 2025 production volume totaled 77.6 billion dollars, up 17 percent year over year. That is not theoretical capacity. That is actual evidence that the agency lane is still active and scaled.

There is also a useful signal from Freddie Mac on structure. In a customer update published April 1, Freddie said it closed seven new long-term financing facilities in 2025 and ended the year with 15 actively managed facilities. That is not the broad market, but it is a good read on what sophisticated sponsors still want in this environment: flexibility, laddered structures, and optionality around how and when collateral gets financed. Borrowers do not just want the lowest coupon anymore. They want a structure that can survive uncertainty.

For live multifamily execution, that means the cleanest stories are still going agency first. If the asset is stabilized or close to stabilized, the agencies remain highly competitive. If the deal needs more runway, more rehab, or more tolerance for uneven near-term NOI, bridge and debt-fund capital are still doing the heavy lifting. What is changed from the easy-money era is that the bridge loan now has to be designed from day one with a real agency or permanent exit in mind. Hope is not a takeout strategy.

CMBS still has a role in multifamily, but more as a selective outlet than a default answer. With newer-vintage multifamily stress now showing up in delinquency and distress data, securitized lenders and bond buyers are watching assumptions more closely. Occupancy trends, tax exposure, insurance, concessions, and reserves all matter. If any part of the story feels thin, borrowers should expect lower proceeds or more structure.

HUD and FHA still deserve a real place in the conversation. HUD announced earlier this year that it would level multifamily mortgage insurance premium categories to 25 basis points, a move meant to make the channel more cost-competitive across programs. That helps on economics, especially for borrowers looking for longer duration, affordability-oriented execution, or a more stable capital stack. But the familiar caveat remains: HUD can still be an excellent answer, just not usually the fastest answer. Timing discipline and process discipline still determine whether FHA feels like a competitive solution or a slow one.

So if you step back, the multifamily market is not short on capital. It is short on patience for fuzzy underwriting. That is a different problem, and it is one better borrowers can solve.

Before we close, here is the markets snapshot.

As of the April 3 Treasury close, the two-year was 3.84 percent, the five-year 3.99 percent, the ten-year 4.17 percent, and the thirty-year 4.91 percent.

The latest published SOFR remains in the mid-3.6 percent range, which keeps floating-rate debt more manageable than late 2025, but still not cheap enough to ignore.

Trepp’s March CMBS delinquency rate is 7.55 percent.

KBRA’s March private-label CMBS delinquency rate is 7.7 percent, with distress at 10.3 percent.

Agency capacity remains large, with 88 billion dollar caps for both Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac in 2026.

And on the macro side, oil shock is back in the conversation after Thursday’s surge, with U.S. benchmark crude last quoted above 111 dollars a barrel and Brent above 109 in Friday market coverage.

One thing to watch next:

Watch whether the Iran conflict changes the front end of the rates story before it changes the long end. If oil stays high and the labor market still looks firm, the market can get trapped between growth anxiety and inflation anxiety at the same time. For commercial real estate debt, that is not a great mix. It can keep Treasury volatility alive, keep lenders cautious on proceeds, and make spread discipline more important than any borrower wants it to be.

The bottom line this morning is that capital is available, but it is being offered on grown-up terms. The national backdrop is more volatile than it was twenty-four hours ago. The Treasury curve is still expensive enough to matter. CMBS is open but selective. Debt funds still have a major role. The agencies remain the cleanest answer for a lot of multifamily. And HUD is still a credible lane when duration matters more than speed.

If you are heading into the new week with a financing decision to make, the winning posture is still realism. Underwrite to slower lease-up. Underwrite to higher operating costs. Underwrite to the possibility that your takeout lender asks harder questions than the current bridge lender. In this market, the deal that closes is usually the one built to survive a little disappointment.

That is your Debt Desk for Saturday, April 4th, 2026.

I will see you Monday.

item.title: Debt Desk — Friday, April 03, 2026
item.description: Debt Desk daily: national news plus commercial real estate debt and multifamily capital markets, including SOFR, Treasury curve moves, CMBS, debt funds, and agency/HUD execution.

item.title: Debt Desk — Thursday, April 02, 2026
item.description: Debt Desk daily: national news plus commercial real estate debt and multifamily capital markets, including SOFR, Treasury curve moves, CMBS, debt funds, and agency/HUD execution.
